does: This paper introduces A-Eval, an application-driven evaluation benchmark designed to help practitioners select the most suitable large language model for their specific use cases. The authors categorize evaluation tasks into five main categories spanning 27 sub-categories based on real-world application needs, construct a dataset of 678 annotated question-answer pairs, and develop an objective evaluation methodology. They benchmark multiple LLMs of varying scales on this framework, revealing empirical relationships between model size, task difficulty, and performance. The work culminates in proposing a practical method for selecting the optimal model that balances task performance with computational cost—a critical concern for production deployments where generic one-size-fits-all evaluation may not reflect actual usage patterns.
connects: This work extends the long-standing challenge of model selection and evaluation in machine learning by addressing limitations of existing LLM benchmarks. Earlier work established general-purpose benchmarks like GLUE and SuperGLUE for evaluating language models on diverse tasks, but these often fail to capture the requirements of practical applications. The paper builds on recent trends in LLM evaluation that move beyond academic metrics toward task-specific and cost-aware assessment. It responds to the proliferation of fine-tuned and RLHF-enhanced models by acknowledging that a single model rarely optimizes all dimensions—performance, latency, and cost. This connects to prior work on model scaling laws and the efficiency-accuracy tradeoff that has become central as LLMs have grown exponentially larger.
enables: By providing a systematic, application-driven evaluation framework with clear guidance on model selection, this work enables practitioners to make data-informed deployment decisions in resource-constrained settings. The revealed relationships between model scale and task difficulty could support future work on dynamic model selection systems that route queries to appropriately-sized models based on complexity. The framework is extensible to new application domains and emerging model architectures, potentially serving as a foundation for standardized industrial evaluation practices. The cost-aware evaluation methodology opens pathways for research on efficient model serving, task-specific adaptation strategies, and automated benchmarking pipelines that could inform both model development and infrastructure planning for organizations deploying LLMs at scale.
